text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Department of Medical Oncology

Patient: Marcus T. Delgado
DOB: 1965
Diagnosis: Stage IV adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon (KRAS G12D, MSS), with bilobar liver metastases and now on maintenance chemotherapy
Attending Oncologist: [Attending Oncologist]
Note Type: Outpatient Follow-Up / Maintenance Therapy Check

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic male with known stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma presenting for routine oncology follow-up approximately two months into maintenance 5-fluorouracil/leucovorin/bevacizumab therapy. He is here today for toxicity assessment, laboratory review, and interval status check, with restaging CT planned in the next six weeks.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Delgado was initially diagnosed with stage IV adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon approximately eight months ago following workup of rectal bleeding and changes in bowel habits. Staging revealed a 4.5 cm sigmoid primary with synchronous bilobar liver metastases (four lesions, largest 3.8 cm in segment VI). Tumor molecular profiling demonstrated KRAS G12D mutation (VAF 42%), rendering him ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy. Additional alterations included APC R1450*, TP53 R248W, SMAD4 R361H, and PIK3CA E545K. Microsatellite status was confirmed MSS with TMB 3.2 mut/Mb. At multidisciplinary tumor board, he was deemed unresectable at presentation and initiated on FOLFOX plus bevacizumab with conversion intent.

He completed twelve cycles of induction FOLFOX/bevacizumab — the last two cycles modified to 5-FU/leucovorin/bevacizumab after oxaliplatin was permanently discontinued at cycle 10 due to grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy (cumulative oxaliplatin dose approximately 760 mg/m2). Best response at completion of induction was a meaningful partial response: the segment VI lesion shrunk from 3.8 to 2.3 cm, segment IVa from 2.1 to 1.2 cm, segment II from 1.3 to 0.7 cm, and segment VII from 0.9 to 0.4 cm. The primary sigmoid tumor decreased from 4.5 to 3.2 cm. CEA improved from 487 ng/mL at diagnosis to 87 ng/mL. CA 19-9 fell from 312 to 64 U/mL. Despite this response, the hepatobiliary surgery team felt that bilobar distribution and concern for adequate future liver remnant precluded conversion resection. The patient was transitioned to maintenance therapy per OPTIMOX1/CAIRO3 strategy.

He is now approximately two months into maintenance 5-FU/LV/bevacizumab. He reports tolerating the regimen reasonably well. He denies any new focal neurologic symptoms, fevers, chills, or rigors. He has not had any hospitalizations since transitioning to maintenance. He returned to part-time teaching duties approximately three weeks ago, which he describes as deeply meaningful to his sense of normalcy and identity.

Regarding his peripheral neuropathy: he reports that the severe bilateral hand and foot numbness and functional impairment that led to oxaliplatin discontinuation has been gradually improving. He no longer drops objects, and the difficulties with fine motor tasks such as buttoning and writing have largely resolved. He still has some persistent tingling and mild numbness primarily in his feet, which bothers him particularly when walking on uneven surfaces — but he can navigate uneven terrain without assistance now. He continues to take gabapentin 300 mg three times daily, which he feels provides partial relief. Cold sensitivity in the fingertips is largely resolved.

His blood pressure has been better controlled. He was managed on amlodipine 5 mg initially, escalated to 10 mg at cycle 8 for grade 2 hypertension related to bevacizumab, and lisinopril 10 mg was subsequently added by his cardiologist. He has been checking blood pressure at home; recent readings have been in the 130s/80s range.

He denies significant nausea or vomiting on the current 5-FU/LV regimen. He has mild hand-foot syndrome — slight skin dryness and tenderness in palms bilaterally, grade 1 — no blistering or ulceration. He denies mucositis. Bowel function has been fairly normal, with one to two formed stools per day, no diarrhea, no rectal bleeding.

His appetite has been fair. He has maintained his weight over the past two months. He notes mild fatigue in the days immediately following infusion, which resolves by day 4-5. His wife, Elena, accompanies him today. He continues to work part-time and participates in his children's events on weekends.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild post-infusion fatigue, resolves within 4-5 days; no significant weight loss; appetite fair; no fevers, chills, or night sweats.
Neurologic: Residual peripheral neuropathy in bilateral feet (tingling, mild numbness), improving from prior grade 3; no new proximal weakness, no falls, no changes in vision or hearing.
Cardiovascular: Hypertension as noted, currently better controlled on dual antihypertensives; no chest pain, palpitations, or dyspnea on exertion; no lower extremity edema.
Pulmonary: No cough, hemoptysis, or shortness of breath.
Gastrointestinal: Normal bowel function; no nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, hematochezia, or melena; mild abdominal pressure sensation occasionally but no acute pain.
Genitourinary: No hematuria, dysuria, or changes in urinary stream; well-controlled type 2 diabetes with no new urinary complaints.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain or joint swelling.
Skin/integument: Grade 1 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, mild dryness; no ulceration; mild alopecia from prior 5-FU (minimal, not bothersome per patient).
Psychiatric: Coping reasonably well; reports good family support; occasional anxiety about next imaging; accepting of treatment plan.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Type 2 diabetes on metformin, home glucose readings 110-145 mg/dL fasting, no hypoglycemic episodes.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Mr. Delgado is a well-appearing, moderately built Hispanic male in no acute distress, conversational, and engaging appropriately. He appears his stated age. He arrived ambulatory without assistive device.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 134/84 mmHg (right arm, seated), heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36.8°C, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight 183 lbs (stable from prior visit, 83.0 kg). Height 5'9" (175 cm). BSA approximately 1.98 m2.

ECOG Performance Status: 1 (ambulatory, capable of light work, spends >50% of waking hours out of bed).

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Anicteric sclerae. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis or oral ulcerations. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular chains.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. No peripheral edema in bilateral lower extremities.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild fullness appreciated in the left lower quadrant corresponding to the known sigmoid primary, non-tender to palpation at this location today. Liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below the right costal margin; non-tender. No splenomegaly. No palpable mass in the right or left upper quadrant. No shifting dullness or fluid wave suggesting ascites. Bowel sounds normal.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or significant edema. Mild skin dryness in bilateral palms consistent with grade 1 PPE; no open sores or fissures.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Gait is steady on a flat surface without assistive device. Heel-to-toe tandem gait mildly impaired (one corrective step needed) consistent with residual proprioceptive deficit. Romberg mildly positive. Sensation to light touch decreased in a stocking pattern to mid-foot bilaterally; vibration sense reduced in bilateral great toes (intact at ankles); pinprick mildly blunted in bilateral feet. Upper extremity sensation largely restored — light touch normal in bilateral fingers; vibration normal at wrists. Deep tendon reflexes: patellar 1+ bilaterally, Achilles absent bilaterally (consistent with axonal sensorimotor neuropathy). No upper motor neuron signs.

Skin: Mild diffuse alopecia (patchy, vertex); no rashes. No skin breakdown or wound concerns.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC (obtained prior to today's visit):
- WBC: 5.9 x10^9/L (reference 4.0-11.0)
- ANC: 3.2 x10^9/L (adequate for treatment)
- Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable)
- Platelets: 214 x10^9/L (adequate)
- MCV: 88 fL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride 103 mEq/L, CO2 25 mEq/L
- BUN 18 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.94 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2)
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, ALT 38 U/L, AST 44 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 112 U/L (mildly elevated, stable vs. prior, consistent with hepatic metastatic burden)
- Albumin 3.7 g/dL
- Glucose 128 mg/dL (non-fasting)

Tumor Markers:
- CEA: 79 ng/mL (down from 87 ng/mL at end of induction, stable and not rising — reassuring)
- CA 19-9: 58 U/mL (down from 64 U/mL at end of induction)

HbA1c (obtained at this visit per diabetes monitoring): 7.2% (acceptable glycemic control)

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1, PTT 28 seconds (normal).

Note: CEA trend over course of treatment: 487 (dx) → 198 (cycle 4) → 87 (end of induction, cycle 12) → 79 (today, 2 months maintenance). Tumor markers continue to reflect disease control without signs of re-acceleration, which is encouraging.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent CT CAP (approximately 2 months prior, at completion of induction therapy): Documented above in HPI. Liver metastases significantly reduced from baseline: segment VI 2.3 cm, segment IVa 1.2 cm, segment II 0.7 cm, segment VII 0.4 cm. Primary sigmoid mass 3.2 cm. No extrahepatic disease at that time.

Next restaging CT CAP is planned in approximately 6 weeks per schedule. No new imaging has been obtained at this visit.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

IMPRESSION:
Mr. Delgado is a 58-year-old male with stage IV left-sided sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS, bilobar hepatic metastases) who has completed 12 cycles of induction FOLFOX/bevacizumab with a meaningful partial response and is now two months into maintenance 5-FU/LV/bevacizumab. He is tolerating maintenance therapy reasonably well with grade 1 PPE, mild post-infusion fatigue, and continued but improving oxaliplatin-related peripheral neuropathy. Tumor markers remain stable with no evidence of rising CEA or CA 19-9, suggesting ongoing disease control.

Active Problems and Plans:

1. STAGE IV SIGMOID COLON ADENOCARCINOMA — DISEASE STABLE ON MAINTENANCE
   - Current regimen: 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours plus leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV plus bevacizumab 7.5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks. Today marks cycle 5 of maintenance.
   - Tumor markers trending favorably: CEA 79 → 79 ng/mL (essentially flat), CA 19-9 64 → 58 U/mL. No biochemical evidence of progression at this time.
   - Next CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 6 weeks for restaging. Will assess hepatic lesions for any interval change. Hepatobiliary surgery has been kept in the loop; we will relay updated imaging to the liver surgery team for reassessment of resectability at each interval.
   - Patient remains ECOG PS 1 and is functioning at a good level, including return to part-time work. This is an important quality-of-life marker.
   - Continue maintenance strategy per current plan. No dose modifications needed today.

2. OXALIPLATIN-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY — IMPROVING
   - At its nadir, neuropathy was grade 3: bilateral hand and foot numbness to wrists/mid-calf with functional impairment (dropping objects, difficulty walking on uneven surfaces). Oxaliplatin was permanently discontinued after cycle 10 (cumulative dose ~760 mg/m2).
   - Today: neuropathy grade 2, improving — residual stocking distribution sensory loss in feet, diminished vibratory sense in great toes, absent Achilles reflexes bilaterally. Upper extremity largely recovered. Functional ADLs intact. Patient ambulates without assist.
   - Neurology previously performed EMG/NCS confirming axonal sensorimotor neuropathy, length-dependent, consistent with oxaliplatin injury. Prognosis for further partial recovery over months is expected, though complete resolution at grade 3 severity is uncertain.
   - Gabapentin 300 mg TID: continue. Patient reports partial benefit for discomfort. May taper gradually as symptoms continue to improve over the next 1-2 months.
   - Continue to monitor. If neuropathy worsens despite not being on oxaliplatin, will reconsider etiology (e.g., paraneoplastic, diabetic component — patient does have type 2 DM, though HbA1c 7.2% is adequately controlled).

3. BEVACIZUMAB-RELATED HYPERTENSION — CONTROLLED
   - History: developed grade 1 hypertension early in FOLFOX/bevacizumab course, progressed to grade 2 at cycle 8, managed with amlodipine 10 mg. Lisinopril 10 mg added subsequently.
   - Today's BP: 134/84 mmHg — well controlled on amlodipine 10 mg and lisinopril 10 mg. No further antihypertensive escalation needed at this time.
   - Will continue bevacizumab at 7.5 mg/kg for maintenance (dose appropriate for maintenance regimen).
   - Remind patient to continue home BP monitoring. If readings exceed 150/95 mmHg on home monitoring, he should contact the clinic.
   - Cardiology co-management ongoing.

4. GRADE 1 PALMAR-PLANTAR ERYTHRODYSESTHESIA (PPE) — 5-FU RELATED
   - Mild skin dryness and tenderness in bilateral palms, grade 1. No blistering, ulceration, or functional impairment. No dosing modification required.
   - Recommend generous use of urea-based emollients (e.g., Udderly Smooth or equivalent) twice daily. Avoid trauma to palms. Avoid hot water.
   - Patient instructed to report worsening to grade 2 (blistering, pain limiting ADLs) promptly.

5. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS — STABLE
   - On metformin (dose continued from prior to diagnosis). HbA1c 7.2%, acceptable under oncology guidelines. Home fasting glucose 110-145 mg/dL, no hypoglycemic episodes.
   - Continue metformin. Endocrinology/primary care managing in parallel. Note: some corticosteroid use as pre-medication may transiently elevate glucose — patient and care team aware.
   - No change to diabetes regimen at this visit.

6. ANEMIA
   - Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, mild normocytic anemia, stable. Likely multifactorial: anemia of chronic disease / cancer-related, some contribution from chemotherapy myelosuppression.
   - No transfusion indicated; patient asymptomatic (no exertional dyspnea, no lightheadedness).
   - Continue monitoring with each cycle CBC. Iron studies and B12/folate were checked at the start of treatment and were normal; no supplementation added.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL / QUALITY OF LIFE
   - Patient has returned to part-time work as a high school teacher. This is a very meaningful quality-of-life milestone for him and reflects preserved functional capacity.
   - Wife Elena is supportive and attends most visits. Two adult children involved in his care.
   - Patient has not yet engaged formal psychosocial support services beyond our nursing team check-ins. He was offered a referral to our oncology social worker at a prior visit; today he agreed to schedule a meeting to explore patient/family support resources.
   - Palliative care was offered at today's visit for concurrent symptom management and advance care planning support. Patient politely declined at this time, stating he feels well-supported and prefers to address that when and if the situation changes. This is noted and will be revisited at future visits.

FOLLOW-UP:
- Next infusion: in 2 weeks (cycle 6, maintenance 5-FU/LV/bevacizumab)
- CBC and CMP to be obtained prior to each cycle
- CEA and CA 19-9 tumor markers: every 4 weeks (next set in approximately 4 weeks)
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: approximately 6 weeks from today for restaging
- Hepatobiliary surgery consult to be re-engaged once updated imaging available
- Follow up with cardiology for ongoing blood pressure management
- Gabapentin taper to be considered over the next 1-2 months if neuropathy continues to improve
- Oncology social work referral placed today

Patient and spouse verbalized understanding of the above plan and expressed agreement. Questions were addressed. Patient in good spirits overall.

Electronically signed by:
[Attending Medical Oncologist]
Department of Medical Oncology
